result:
The rain doesn't fall here anymore. Instead, the sky exhales something between mist and memory, a soft silver that clings to eyelashes and old regrets. I watched it this morning from the roof of an abandoned library, legs dangling over the edge like the last two footnotes in a forgotten book.

There's something magnificent about cities that have given up pretending. The billboards have faded into gentle ghosts—smiling women from toothpaste ads now look like saints offering absolution for bad breath. Concrete cracks bloom with flowers that shouldn't exist this far north. Nature doesn't reclaim these places so much as it forgives them.

I met a man yesterday who collects silences. He says the best ones come right after laughter dies, those three perfect seconds where joy and its absence touch. He keeps them in mason jars like fireflies. When he opens one at night, you can hear children who grew up decades ago. The sound makes old women cry in their sleep, but they wake up smiling.

Sometimes I think we're all just elaborate excuses the universe made to experience its own loneliness. Every love song, every war, every time someone burns toast at 2 a.m. while crying over someone who left—it's all just the cosmos trying to understand what it feels like to miss yourself.

The mist is thickening now. I should probably go inside, but the roof feels like the only honest place left. Down there they're still pretending the world makes sense. Up here, we know better.

(Word count: 248)
